content.full_text:
file NOTICE REQUIREMENT-SUFFICIENCY 87311
State of California

Memorandum

To : Ted                                                    Date : November 21, 1977


                                                           m: 77-514

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Dwight

Subject: Franchise Tax Board's Notice Requirements Under the California
         Administrative Procedure Act


        The Franchise Tax Board (FTB) when adopting its Conflict

of Interest Code failed to have it published in the California

Administrative Register. It is disputed whether the proposed

Code was ever sent over to the Office of Administrative

Hearings who prepares the register.

        The question presented is whether the failure of the

FTB to have its Code published violates the California

Administrative Procedure Act (CAPA).

Conclusion

        If the FTB can demonstrate that it sent the Code over

to the Office of Administrative Hearings for publication,

FTB will have satisfied the notice provisions of CAPA, even

if the Code failed to be published. Assuming, that the

FTB failed to send the Code over for publication, an argument

may still be made that they substantially complied with CAPA.

Analysis

        If the FTB provided copies of its proposed rules to the

Office of Administrative Hearings for the purposes of publication

Part Two
November 21, 1977

the FTB will have satisfied Government Code §11423(f) which
states:

     At least 30 days prior to the adoption, amendment
     or repeal of a regulation, notice of the proposed
     action shall be:

     (f) Delivered to the Office of Administrative
     Hearings for publication in the next issue of
     the California Administrative Register as pre-
     pared by that office... ([illegible])

     The section does not state that the regulation must

actually be published before it would be deemed that the

agency has complied with the notice provisions of CAPA.

The section only calls for the agency to "deliver" the

proposal for publication.  If the FTB delivered the Code

for the purpose of publication, it has complied with the

provisions of the section.

     Assuming that the FTB never sent over the proposed

rule for publication, an argument can still be made that

the FTB substantially complied with the notice provisions

of CAPA.  The FTB followed all of the other steps listed

for the purpose of providing notice to all interested indi-

viduals.  The notice of proposed rule-making provision of

CAPA (Government Code §11423) calls for the agency to publish

the proposal in a newspaper of general circulation, file the

proposal with certain legislative committees, mail a copy to

every person who requested notice and to those who might be

interested.  FTB complied with all of those steps.

Ted
Page Three
November 21, 1977

The purpose behind providing notice is to insure full

public participation in the decision-making process. The

court is also concerned with a record being present for

assuring effective judicial review. California Optometric
Assn. v. Lackner 60 Cal. App. 3d 500,506 (1976).

The FTB may be able to argue that it did present oppor-

tunities for and had full public participation when it adopted

its code, notwithstanding the fact that the Code wasn't

published in the register. The court in dicta stated:

"Administrators may indulge in rule-making with fair assurance

of procedural legality by inviting as much public participation

as time and staff permit." California Optometric Assn. v.

Lackner 60 Cal. App. 3d 500,511 (1976).

If the agency can demonstrate that it had lots of public

input, it may be able to argue that it substantially complied

with CAPA. The court when reviewing agency decision-making

is bound to follow that type of standard. Government Code

§11440 states:

Any interested person may obtain a judicial
declaration as to the validity of any regu-
lation by bringing an action for declaratory
relief...Such regulation may be declared to
be invalid for a substantial failure to comply
with the provisions of this chapter...
[Emphasis added.]

The facts, as I understand them, are that the FTB had

full and open proceedings when it adopted its Code. It's

doubtful that publication of the Code in the register would

have led to any more participation by the public in a

Ted
Page Four
November 21, 1977

significant way.  Although there is no case law directly on
point, it is doubtful that the court would throw out the Code
because of its technical failure to fully comply with the
notice provision.

     The PTB may be able to argue that all those affected
had actual notice and could have participated in the rule-
making, even if they did not do so.  The Federal APA, on which
CAPA is based, and federal case law, actual notice is
sufficient "even if the proposed rule was not published as
required by statute.  The Federal Administrative Procedure
Act states:

          General notice of proposed rule-making shall
          be published in the Federal Register, unless
          persons subject thereto are named and other
          personally served or otherwise have actual
          notice thereof in accordance with law.
          5 U.S.C.A.
                                        [Emphasis added.]

     CAPA's notice provisions does not contain the language
concerning actual notice.  However, the argument should be
made that if actual notice is present, then the purposes for
which publication is required has been satisfied and the point
should follow the federal standard.


DD:ma

sections.question_synthetic: Does the Franchise Tax Board's failure to publish its Conflict of Interest Code in the California Administrative Register violate CAPA?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that if FTB sent the Code to the Office of Administrative Hearings for publication, it satisfied CAPA notice requirements regardless of actual publication. Even if it failed to send the Code, FTB may argue substantial compliance with CAPA if it followed other notice procedures and provided public participation opportunities. A court would likely not invalidate the Code for this technical procedural failure.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document is significantly degraded with OCR errors and missing text. Some sections are illegible or incomplete. The core question and conclusion sections were extractable but with moderate confidence due to text quality issues. Document references Government Code §11423(f) and case law including California Medical Assn. v. Lackner.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter analyzing whether the Franchise Tax Board's failure to publish its Conflict of Interest Code in the California Administrative Register violates CAPA. The FPPC concludes that FTB likely satisfied or substantially complied with notice requirements under CAPA.
